arabic: أَمْسَيْنَا وَأَمْسَى الْمُلْكُ لِلَّهِ وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ، لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ لَا شَرِيكَ لَهُ
transliteration: Amsayna wa amsal mulku lillah, walhamdu lillah, la ilaha illallahu wahdahu la sharika lah
translation: We have entered the evening and all sovereignty belongs to Allah. Praise be to Allah. None has the right to be worshipped except Allah, alone, with no partner.

insights.meaning: The evening counterpart of the morning dhikr — closing the day with the same affirmation of Allah's sovereignty.
insights.benefits: Seals the day with gratitude and Tawheed, creating spiritual continuity from dawn to dusk.
insights.when: After Asr prayer, as the evening begins.
insights.explanation: Just as we open the day affirming Allah's ownership of all things, we close it with the same beautiful declaration.

arabic: اللَّهُمَّ بِكَ أَمْسَيْنَا، وَبِكَ أَصْبَحْنَا، وَبِكَ نَحْيَا، وَبِكَ نَمُوتُ، وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ
transliteration: Allahumma bika amsayna, wa bika asbahna, wa bika nahya, wa bika namutu, wa ilaykal-masir
translation: O Allah, by You we enter the evening, by You we enter the morning, by You we live, by You we die, and to You is the return.

insights.meaning: The evening version of the morning supplication — noting the end is 'masir' (return to Allah) rather than 'nushur' (resurrection).
insights.benefits: A powerful reminder at day's end that our ultimate destination is Allah.
insights.explanation: The subtle change in wording between morning and evening versions reflects the Islamic understanding of returning to Allah at the end of each day.
